votes.reason: The Python code correctly implements the primality check by testing divisibility for all integers strictly between 1 and n, matching the Coq specification.
The Python code correctly implements the mathematical definition of divisibility and primality checking as specified in the Coq code.
The Python code correctly implements the divides check and the primality test, returning whether the boolean b matches the computed primality of n, which exactly mirrors the Coq specification.

The Python code correctly implements primality checking. The divides function checks divisibility (handling d=0 edge case appropriately), and is_prime_spec returns True iff b matches whether n is prime. The Coq spec says b=true iff n>1 and no d in (1,n) divides n, which is exactly what the Python computes: is_p is True when n>1 and no divisor found in range(2,n). The final comparison b == is_p matches the biconditional in Coq.

The Python code correctly implements primality checking: divides(d, n) returns True iff d divides n (handling d=0 correctly), and is_prime_spec(n, b) returns True iff b matches whether n is prime (n > 1 and no divisor in range 2 to n-1). This matches the Coq spec where b = true iff (1 < n and no d with 1 < d < n divides n).

The Python code correctly implements primality checking. The divides function checks divisibility (d divides n iff n % d == 0, with special case for d=0). The is_prime_spec function returns true iff b matches whether n is prime, where prime means n > 1 and no d in range 2 to n-1 divides n. This matches the Coq spec: b = true iff (1 < n and for all d with 1 < d < n, d does not divide n).
Python computes primality as n>1 with no divisors d in 2..n-1 and returns b == that result; divides matches Coq (including d=0 case).
Python divides matches Coq’s exists-k definition (including d=0 case), and is_prime_spec computes primality (n>1 and no 2..n-1 divisors) then checks b equals that, aligning with b=true <-> primality.

Python divides matches Coq’s definition (including d=0), and is_prime_spec computes primality by checking n>1 and no divisors 2..n-1, then returns b == primality. This makes the Python result true exactly when the Coq equivalence (b = true <-> primality) holds for all n.
